text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic dermoid cystectomy by inserting a Veress needle into the inferior crease of the umbilicus. After confirming entry into the abdominal cavity with irrigation, suction, and the hanging drop test, pneumoperitoneum is established using 15 mmHg pressure with 1.5 liters of gas. The incision is then enlarged to 11 mm for the introduction of an 11 mm optical trocar.

The surgeon dilates the inferior crease of the umbilicus and introduces an 11 mm trocar. After connecting the tubing and increasing the pressure, a telescope is inserted, revealing the sigmoid colon, uterus, and a dermoid cyst on the left side. An endo bag is introduced through the contralateral port to encapsulate the cyst and ovary, preventing spillage during the cystectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the left side of the triangle of dome, to prevent interference during the procedure. Using a harmonic scalpel, the outer ovarian cortex is opened, and the dermoid cyst is carefully dissected with a Maryland dissector and atraumatic grasper. The surgeon avoids directly holding the cyst with the Maryland and ensures gentle dissection around the cyst to prevent puncture, while avoiding the fimbria of the fallopian tube to prevent stricture formation.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to hold the ovarian cortex with the left hand while gently separating the dermoid cyst from the surrounding tissue inside the endobag. The cyst is slowly stripped from all sides to minimize the risk of spillage, ensuring careful and equal traction.

The surgeon carefully encircles the ovarian cyst, ensuring equal dissection around its circumference to avoid puncturing. Approximately 25% of the cyst remains attached, and the surgeon gently separates the last part without causing bleeding. The fimbria is visible, and care is taken to avoid contact with it during the separation from the ovarian cortex.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation within the cystic area to wash and visualize the site, confirming the absence of bleeding.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to desiccate small bleeding points, inverting the outer wall of the ovarian cortex and dropping it into the ovarian fossa. The cyst is punctured inside the endo bag using harmonic energy to prevent spillage. The ovum forceps are used to drag out the cystic content, including fatty material and hair balls from the dermoid cyst, ensuring the endo bag port is closed to prevent hernia.
